ground_truth_qa.reviewer_notes: Textbook call. Marcus introduced himself clearly, collected both full legal name and date of birth before accessing any account information, and stated the purpose of the interaction. Empathy was natural and consistent throughout — he acknowledged the patient's stress, validated her concern about the unexpected charge, and never sounded scripted or dismissive. On accuracy, he gathered the statement date, account number, CPT code, and visit context before drawing any conclusions; critically, he correctly identified the limits of his role and escalated to the billing team rather than making a clinical coding determination himself. Protocol adherence was flawless: identity first, balance held during dispute, escalation ticket created with a reference number. The closing was exemplary — he recapped every action taken, provided the specific reference number twice, explained the 7-10 business day timeline, named the follow-up channel (patient portal or callback), and closed warmly. No gaps identified.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: Thank you for calling HealthBridge, this is Marcus speaking. How can I help you today?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Hi Marcus, my name is Patricia Holloway. I'm calling because I got a bill in the mail that seems really off to me. I was expecting maybe a small copay but this says I owe over $400 and I have no idea why.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: I completely understand how unsettling it can be to receive an unexpected bill like that, Patricia. I'd be happy to look into this with you. Before I can access your account, I do need to verify your identity first. Could you please confirm your full legal name and your date of birth for me?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Sure, it's Patricia Anne Holloway, and my date of birth is March 14th, 1978.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: Thank you, Patricia. I've confirmed your identity — welcome to your account. I can see a few recent claims here. Can you tell me which bill you're looking at? Does it have a statement date or an account number on it?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Yes, the statement date is May 8th, 2025, and the account number on it is TW-2047839. The charge is $412.50 and it's listed as — let me read this — 'Office Visit Level 4, CPT 99214' from my appointment on April 22nd. I only went in for a blood pressure check. I've had that same annual check-in for three years and I've never paid more than my $30 copay.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: Thank you for reading that off, Patricia. I really do hear your frustration — a $412.50 charge when you're used to a $30 copay is a significant difference and it absolutely warrants a close look. Let me pull up that claim now. I'm looking at the April 22nd visit with Dr. Renata Singh, and I can see the claim was billed under CPT code 99214, which is a Level 4 established patient office visit. That's a moderately complex visit code. Can I ask — did you discuss anything beyond the blood pressure check during that appointment? For example, a medication change, a new concern, or a review of lab results?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: I mean, Dr. Singh did briefly mention my Lisinopril dosage and we talked about my cholesterol numbers for maybe five minutes, but I wouldn't call that a full complex visit. It felt like a routine check-in to me. Could they have coded it wrong?

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: That's a very reasonable question, and honestly, what you're describing — a brief medication review and a quick lab discussion in the context of a blood pressure check-in — does sound like it could potentially be a coding discrepancy. I want to be straightforward with you: I'm not clinically qualified to make a final determination on whether the code was correct, and that determination really needs to come from our certified billing specialists who can review the provider's documentation. What I can do right now is document everything you've told me and escalate this as a formal coding review request to our billing department. They are the right team to resolve this, and they will compare the visit notes against the billed CPT code.

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Okay, yes, please do that. I just want to make sure I'm not being overcharged. And what happens to the $412.50 in the meantime? Am I supposed to pay it?

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: Great question. When I submit this as a formal billing dispute, I'm going to flag your account so that the balance of $412.50 is placed on a temporary hold — that means no late fees will accrue and this will not go to collections while the review is in process. You are not required to pay that amount until the review is complete. The billing team typically resolves coding review disputes within 7 to 10 business days. You'll receive a written notice either by mail or through your patient portal — I see you're enrolled — confirming the outcome. If the code is corrected, your balance will be adjusted accordingly, likely back to your standard $30 copay. If the original code is upheld, you'll receive a clear explanation of why, and we can discuss payment options at that point.

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: That's really helpful, thank you. So I don't need to do anything right now?

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: Correct — you don't need to take any action today. I'm submitting the escalation ticket right now. Your dispute reference number is BDR-2025-110482. I'd recommend saving that number in case you want to follow up. You can call us back and reference that number, or you can check the status in your patient portal under 'Billing Inquiries.' Is there anything else on this bill or any other account matter I can help you with today?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: No, I think that covers it. I really appreciate you explaining everything so clearly. I was pretty stressed when I called.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: I completely understand, and I'm really glad we could give you some clarity and peace of mind. To recap what we've done today: I verified your identity, reviewed the April 22nd claim billed under CPT 99214 for $412.50, placed that balance on a dispute hold so no collections or late fees will occur, and escalated a formal coding review — your reference number is BDR-2025-110482. You should hear back within 7 to 10 business days. Thank you so much for calling HealthBridge, Patricia. Take care and have a great rest of your day.

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Thank you, Marcus. Goodbye.

_scenario: rep verifies identity, gathers full info, explains charges clearly, AND correctly escalates a coding-error dispute to billing — textbook completeness

ground_truth_qa.reviewer_notes: Greeting & Identity: Rep did collect both full name and date of birth before accessing the account, which meets the basic verification requirement — score is a 3 rather than lower because verification was completed correctly, though the rep did not formally state the purpose of the call upfront. Empathy & Tone: Marcus was polite and acknowledged frustration, but the empathy felt hollow given his repeated deflection of a legitimate dispute; he never truly validated the patient's correct assertion that the billing office — not the insurer — was responsible for a coding error. Accuracy & Completeness: This is a clear failure. The patient correctly identified a suspected coding error (CPT 45378 billed alongside 99213 for what should have been a routine preventive visit), which is a provider-side billing issue. The rep should have documented the dispute formally, placed a collections hold, and escalated to a billing specialist or coding review team. Instead, he repeatedly told the patient to call their insurer — which is factually incorrect guidance for a provider-side coding dispute. Score 1. Protocol Adherence: A coding dispute of this nature requires internal escalation per standard billing dispute workflow. The rep failed to initiate a formal dispute ticket, failed to flag the account for collections hold, and failed to transfer or schedule a callback from a billing specialist. This is a protocol failure. Score 1. Closing & Next Steps: Rep offered only a vague note on the account with no ticket number, no timeline, no collections hold confirmation, and no concrete next steps beyond directing the patient to her insurer. The close was premature and left the patient with no resolution path. Score 2.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: Thank you for calling HealthBridge, this is Marcus speaking. How can I help you today?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Hi, yes, I'm calling about a bill I received. It's for a visit back in September and the amount looks completely wrong. I was charged for a procedure I didn't even have.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: I'm sorry to hear that, that sounds frustrating. Can I get your name please?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Sure, it's Linda Hartwell.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: Thank you, Linda. And can you give me your date of birth as well?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Yes, it's March 14th, 1968.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: Perfect, I've got you pulled up here. So you said the bill looks wrong — can you tell me what you're looking at?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Yeah, I have a bill dated October 5th for a visit on September 18th. It says I owe $412 for a colonoscopy prep procedure, CPT code 99213 and some other code, 45378 I think. But I only came in for a routine annual physical. I never had a colonoscopy or any prep for one. This has to be a coding error.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: I see, okay. Yes, I can see the claim here. It does show those two codes billed together. I understand that's confusing.

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: It's more than confusing — it's wrong. Someone billed the wrong codes. Can you fix that? I shouldn't owe $412 for something I never had done.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: I completely understand your concern, Linda, and I'm sorry about the hassle. Unfortunately what I'd recommend is reaching out directly to your insurance company — they can investigate the claim on their end and dispute it if necessary. They have a member services number on the back of your insurance card.

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Wait, shouldn't you be the ones fixing this? It's your office that billed the wrong code, not my insurance company. Why would I call them?

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: I hear you, and I know it's not ideal. It's just that once the claim goes to insurance, they're really the ones who process it. Your insurer would be best positioned to handle a dispute like this. I'm not really able to change the coding on my end.

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Can you at least flag it or put a note on my account or escalate it to someone in billing who can actually look at this? I don't want this sent to collections while I'm waiting.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: I can put a note on your account that you called in about it, which is good. And I don't think it would go to collections right away — you usually have some time. But the fastest path to resolving this really would be contacting your insurer. I'm sorry I can't do more today.

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: This is really disappointing. I expected you to be able to help me with this.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: I understand, and I'm truly sorry for the frustration. Is there anything else I can help you with today?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: No. I guess not.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: Okay, thank you for calling HealthBridge, Linda. Have a good day.

_scenario: rep verifies identity and is polite but fails to escalate a clear coding-error dispute — tells the patient to contact their insurer themselves

ground_truth_qa.reviewer_notes: Greeting & Identity: Rep collected both full name and date of birth before acting, which satisfies the minimum verification requirement. Score of 4 rather than 5 because the rep immediately moved to act on the pharmacy record without first confirming it with the patient — a missed clarifying step that the patient had to catch. Empathy & Tone: Marcus was warm, apologetic when the error surfaced, and validated Diana's frustration throughout. Tone was consistently professional. Minor deduction because the initial eagerness to act contributed to the error, but recovery was handled well. Accuracy & Completeness: The rep sent (or nearly sent) the refill to an outdated pharmacy without confirming it first — a clear accuracy failure. The error was caught and corrected before full submission, the correct pharmacy was updated in the system, and urgency was flagged, so recovery was solid. Score of 3 reflects the initial failure offset by a reasonable recovery. Protocol Adherence: Identity verification was completed correctly, but best practice for refill workflows is to confirm the patient's current pharmacy before submitting any request. The rep skipped that step and had to be corrected by the patient. Score of 3 reflects the procedural miss that was later remediated. Closing & Next Steps: Excellent closing — Marcus clearly summarized all three actions taken (pharmacy updated, refill submitted with urgency note, profile corrected), gave Diana a concrete action item (call Walgreens if no response in a few hours), and closed warmly. Full marks.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: Thank you for calling HealthBridge, my name is Marcus. How can I help you today?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Hi Marcus, yes, I need to get a refill on my Lisinopril. I've been trying to get this sorted for a few days now and I'm almost out.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: I'm sorry to hear you've been waiting on this — let's get that taken care of right away. Can I start by getting your full name and date of birth to pull up your account?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Sure, it's Diana Kowalski, date of birth March 14th, 1971.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: Perfect, thank you Diana. Let me pull up your account here... Okay, I'm looking at your profile. I can see you have a prescription for Lisinopril 10 milligrams. I'm going to go ahead and send that refill request over to your pharmacy — looks like we have CVS on Maple Avenue on file for you. I'll get that submitted right now.

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Wait, no — I switched pharmacies about three months ago. I go to the Walgreens on Ridgewood Boulevard now. The CVS on Maple closed down, actually. Please don't send it there.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: Oh, I apologize for that, Diana. I should have confirmed the pharmacy with you before submitting. Let me check — I haven't fully submitted it yet, so I can update the pharmacy before it goes through. Can you give me the full address for the Walgreens on Ridgewood Boulevard so I make sure I have the right location?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Yeah, it's 4820 Ridgewood Boulevard. I think it's in the Eastfield area.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: Got it, 4820 Ridgewood Boulevard. Let me search for that in our system... Yes, I'm finding a Walgreens at that address. I'm going to update your preferred pharmacy on file to this location and then send the refill for Lisinopril 10 milligrams there. Does that sound right?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Yes, that's correct. And can you tell me approximately how long it'll take for them to have it ready? I only have maybe two days of pills left.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: Totally understandable, and I'm glad you caught that pharmacy issue. Once I send the request, the pharmacy typically processes it within two to four hours, though it can vary. Given that you're running low, I'd recommend calling Walgreens directly after we hang up to let them know it's urgent — they may be able to prioritize it. I'll note the urgency in the request as well.

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Okay, that makes sense. I just wish this had been caught earlier, it's been a bit stressful.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: I completely understand, Diana, and I'm sorry for any added stress. The pharmacy info on our end was outdated and that's something we should have prompted you to confirm sooner. I've updated your record now so future refills will go to the correct Walgreens. Is there anything else I can help you with today?

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: No, I think that covers it. Just hoping it goes smoothly this time.

transcript.speaker: rep
transcript.text: Absolutely, and I hope so too. To recap: I've updated your preferred pharmacy to Walgreens at 4820 Ridgewood Boulevard, submitted the refill request for your Lisinopril 10 milligrams with an urgency note, and updated your profile so this doesn't happen again. Please do call Walgreens directly if you haven't heard back within a few hours. Take care, Diana, and have a good rest of your day.

transcript.speaker: patient
transcript.text: Alright, thanks Marcus. Bye.

_scenario: rep initially sends the refill to the wrong pharmacy on outdated info; patient catches it and rep corrects it — initial accuracy error that was recovered
